notes.title: TYPE 1 VS TYPE 2 DIABETES
notes.subject: Pathology
notes.description: Differences between Type 1 and Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.
notes.content:
Type 1 Diabetes happens when the immune system mistakenly attacks and destroys the insulin-producing beta cells in the pancreas. As a result, the body produces little or no insulin, so glucose cannot enter cells properly and remains high in the blood.

Type 2 Diabetes usually happens because the body becomes resistant to insulin. The pancreas may still produce insulin, but the body does not respond to it well. Over time, insulin production may also decrease.

The key difference is this: Type 1 is mainly an autoimmune destruction of beta cells, while Type 2 is mainly insulin resistance.

Type 1 usually begins in children or young adults and requires insulin treatment. Type 2 is more common in adults and is strongly linked with obesity, poor diet, lack of exercise, and family history.

Both types can cause high blood sugar and complications affecting the eyes, kidneys, nerves, heart, and blood vessels if not controlled.
